articles.title: Open Thread
articles.content:

主要是法学教授 | 偶尔持不同意见 | 常为自由意志主义者 | 始终保持独立
你有什么想法？
尤金·沃洛克
|
2026年7月12日 凌晨3:00
下一篇：《黑袍不等于智慧》
尤金·沃洛克是斯坦福大学胡佛研究所的托马斯·M·西贝尔高级研究员，同时也是加州大学洛杉矶分校法学院荣誉退休的加里·T·施瓦茨杰出法学教授和杰出研究教授。当然，他在这里的文章（与其他博主的文章一样）仅代表个人观点，不代表任何机构的立场。他也是“言论自由不沉默”播客的联合主持人。

articles.title: "A Black Robe Is No Guarantee of Gray Matter"
articles.content:
第五巡回法院法官James Ho在拒绝全院重审Lopez v. Ramirez案时发表的异议意见中有一段精彩论述：

我感谢Smith法官提醒我们关注此案，也认同他对法院拒绝全院重审的失望。调解令本应足以确立我们对这份基于驳回资格免疫裁决提起的中间上诉的管辖权……

最后一点：Smith法官在其异议意见的脚注6中指出，[他认为应予重审的合议庭意见]援引了本院在De Leon v. Munoz案（2025 WL 957500，第五巡回法院）中“同样有误”的未公开判决。

我参与了那份未公开判决。需明确的是，该判决在De Leon案中认定上诉法院具有管辖权——这与Smith法官和我在此案中力主的结论一致。然而，该全体裁定意见的脚注2另行暗示，该案早期阶段不存在上诉管辖权。

若脚注2确有错误，那也只能如此。当我们的判决出现错误时——无论是在有先例效力判决的明确判理中，还是在未公开全体裁定意见的脚注旁白中——都令人遗憾。但错误确实会发生。因为法官也是人，而人皆会犯错。黑色法袍不能保证判断无误。我乐于承认错误。

作为法官，我们应努力坦诚面对错误，并寻求纠正的机会。任何声称自己从未犯错的法官，都是我不会信任的法官。

承认错误并不可耻。不承认错误才是可耻的……

《“黑色法袍不能保证判断无误”》一文首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
A nice line from Fifth Circuit Judge James Ho in his dissent from denial of rehearing en banc Thursday in Lopez v. Ramirez:

I'm grateful to Judge Smith [who wrote the principal dissent from denial of rehearing] for flagging this case for our attention, and share his dismay that the court is denying rehearing en banc. The mediation order should have been sufficient to establish appellate jurisdiction over this interlocutory appeal from the denial of qualified immunity….

A final point: In footnote 6 of his dissent, Judge Smith notes that the [panel opinion, which he is arguing should be reheard -EV] found support in our court's "equally errant" unpublished decision in De Leon v. Munoz, 2025 WL 957500 (5th Cir.).

I joined that unpublished decision. That decision, to be clear, found appellate jurisdiction in De Leon—the same result that both Judge Smith and I urge here. That said, footnote 2 of the per curiam decision separately suggests that there was no appellate jurisdiction at an earlier stage in the case.

If footnote 2 was wrong, so be it. It's unfortunate when errors arise in our decisions—whether in express holdings in precedential decisions, or footnote asides in unpublished per curiam rulings. But errors happen. Because judges are human—and humans make mistakes. A black robe is no guarantee of gray matter. I am happy to admit error.

As judges, we should strive to be open about our mistakes, and look for opportunities to correct them. Any judge who claims that he or she has never made a mistake is a judge I wouldn't trust.

There's no shame in admitting error. There's only shame in not admitting error….

The post "A Black Robe Is No Guarantee of Gray Matter" appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: World Professional Association for Transgender Health (WPATH) Can't Go to D.C. Federal Court to Block FTC Enforcement in Texas Federal Court
articles.content:
华盛顿特区联邦法院首席法官詹姆斯·博斯伯格昨日就世界跨性别健康专业协会诉联邦贸易委员会案作出裁决：

当当事人寻求一项特殊救济时，必须做出特殊的展示。经过紧急简报和昨日听证后，法院认为，原告世界跨性别健康专业协会（WPATH）未能就请求临时限制令以阻止联邦贸易委员会在另一 forum 执行的行动做出上述展示。因此，法院将驳回WPATH的临时限制令申请。

根据《联邦民事诉讼规则》第65(b)条，获得临时限制令的申请人必须表明：（1）其“很可能在实体问题上胜诉”；（2）在没有初步救济的情况下“很可能遭受不可弥补的损害”；（3）“利益平衡有利于申请人”；以及（4）该命令“符合公共利益”。

今年早些时候，WPATH在本院起诉联邦贸易委员会，指控委员会的调查活动侵犯了其第一修正案权利。在该诉讼中，本院于5月部分批准了WPATH的初步禁令动议，暂停 FTC “发布”的“已送达的民事调查要求（CID）”的“实施或执行”。该CID要求WPATH提供大量内部记录。法院认定，WPATH已表明很可能在以下主张的实体问题上胜诉：FTC 发出 CID 是为了报复 WPATH 受宪法保护的言论，且原告若没有初步救济将面临不可弥补的损害。然而，法院拒绝给予WPATH更广泛的救济，将其裁定限定于产生该特定 CID 的事实。

联邦贸易委员会和几个州随后在德克萨斯州北区对WPATH提起了单独的执法诉讼。他们指控WPATH违反了《联邦贸易委员会法》关于不公平或欺骗性贸易行为和虚假广告的禁止性规定。WPATH回到本院，请求临时限制令，禁止FTC在德克萨斯州推进诉讼。原告主张，FTC的诉讼涉及的主题与其先前在本院提起的执法前挑战完全相同，且会挫败已存在的初步禁令。原告认为，FTC应在本院而非德克萨斯州北区进行诉讼，并寻求相应的反诉讼禁令。

WPATH 寻求紧急救济的理由之一是，阻止FTC在德克萨斯州北区的进展对于保护本院对先前禁令的管辖权是必要的。哥伦比亚特区巡回法院已裁定，“保护原审法院自身管辖权”的反诉讼禁令比“唯一目的是摧毁”另一法院管辖权的禁令更容易获得正当理由。因此，如果申请人能够证明反诉讼禁令是维护法院管辖权所必需的，则紧急救济可能是正当的。

WPATH尚未证明存在此类威胁。法院认为，从单独的德克萨斯州诉讼来看，不存在“挫败本院已发布初步禁令”的可能性。无论当事人如何描述在德克萨斯州北区的持续诉讼，这都不是执行 CID 的尝试，因为FTC已撤回了该 CID。而且，如上所述，本院唯一的裁定仅限于 CID。

WPATH指出，FTC在德克萨斯州的诉讼攻击了其某些声明，这些声明与CID针对的“涵盖声明”惊人地相似，这可能在德克萨斯州引发对完全相同信息的调查。但本院的禁令并未保护WPATH免于遵守所有信息寻求程序——仅保护其免于遵守法院认定可能是报复性的 CID。本院保留考虑任何当事人提出的未来事项的能力。因此，不同法院的不同诉讼程序不会威胁本院的管辖权。

WPATH 为紧急救济阻止德克萨斯州诉讼提出的其余理由——即FTC的诉讼与本院的诉讼重复——寻求的是不涉及管辖权问题的反诉讼禁令。因此，需要极具说服力的展示才能证明本院在紧急基础上、在简缩简报和审议的情况下干扰同级地区法院的诉讼程序是正当的。

WPATH尚未做出此类展示。法院怀疑《联邦民事诉讼规则》第13(a)条——要求当事人在初始诉讼中作为强制反诉提出相关请求——或避免重复诉讼的理由是否适用于政府执法行动。多家法院认为，在原告提起执法前挑战以阻止FTC获取信息后，不能强迫委员会在原告选择的 forum 中将后续执法行动作为强制反诉提出。

正如法院在昨日听证会上提到的，将FTC的诉讼与执法前调查挑战相联系，可能允许原告“仅仅通过提起执法前诉讼来选择诉讼 forum 和节奏”。法院一直犹豫是否强迫政府机构遵守会压缩其调查时间表并迫使其做出仓促诉讼决定的反诉要求。因此，本院不准备得出结论认为，涉及“主张国会授权执法计划下权利”的诉讼类似于应合理合并在一个 forum 和时间解决的当事人之间的私人纠纷。而在一方法院采取行动终止另一方法院管辖权之前，必须有确实的根据。

法院得出结论认为不需要立即采取行动，这一结论因WPATH无法证明其当前面临不可弥补的损害而得到加强。临时限制令“是一项紧急程序，仅在申请人需要立即救济时才适当”。为了在法院能够充分确定各方立场的是非曲直之前采取预防行动，损害必须影响“重大”且到来“确定”。

WPATH引用了两个损害来源：德克萨斯州诉讼对其第一修正案权利的侵犯，以及“进行两起独立诉讼”产生的金钱损害。然而，为了认定第一种损害的存在，本院必须认定在德克萨斯州北区被起诉本身即是WPATH第一修正案权利的侵犯——否则不会产生损害。

虽然WPATH将其论点建立在法院5月的裁决上，但该裁定仅扩展至其面前的事实：FTC向WPATH发出的广泛 CID 缺乏正当理由。法院在5月并未准备对FTC未来行动的每一种可能情况都预先判定为违宪报复，现在也不打算这样做。没有这样的认定，WPATH仅受制于实体在政府机构对其提起诉讼时的标准诉讼义务——即根本没有损害。

两起诉讼的费用也不足以支持紧急救济。两起诉讼无疑对WPATH造成了负担。然而，初步救济需要重大的金钱损失。“在漫长的裁决程序中为自己辩护的费用和干扰不是不可弥补的损害。”本院的诉讼也尚未发展到在德克萨斯州的诉讼会构成“恶意重审已决问题”的程度。正如法院在昨日听证会上所指出的，WPATH 可在德克萨斯州诉讼中援引本院先前的意见，以阻止证据开示……

约翰·贝利和丹尼尔·F·穆莫洛（司法部民事司）以及亚历克斯·波塔波夫、伊桑·D·贝克、乔纳森·亚伯拉罕·科恩和罗斯·库珀·沃恩（联邦贸易委员会）代表被告。

《世界跨性别健康专业协会（WPATH）无法在华盛顿特区联邦法院阻止德克萨斯州联邦法院的 FTC 执法行动》一文首次发表于 Reason.com。
articles.summary:
From Chief Judge James Boasberg (D.D.C.) yesterday in World Prof. Ass'n for Transgender Health v. FTC:

When parties seek an extraordinary remedy, they must make an extraordinary showing. After expedited briefing and a hearing yesterday, the Court finds that Plaintiff World Professional Association for Transgender Health has not made that showing in its request for a Temporary Restraining Order to block the Federal Trade Commission's enforcement action in a different forum. It therefore will deny WPATH's Motion for Temporary Restraining Order.

To obtain a TRO under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 65(b), a movant must show that (1) it "is likely to succeed on the merits"; (2) it "is likely to suffer irreparable harm in the absence of preliminary relief"; (3) "the balance of equities tips in [the movant's] favor"; and (4) such order "is in the public interest."

Earlier this year, WPATH sued the FTC in this Court alleging that the Commission's investigative efforts violated its First Amendment rights. In that challenge, this Court in May partially granted WPATH's Motion for a Preliminary Injunction halting the "implement[ation] or enforc[ement of]" the "issued CID [Civil Investigative Demand]" that the FTC had served on WPATH seeking a broad range of internal records. The Court held that WPATH had shown that it was likely to succeed on the merits of its claim that the FTC issued the CID in retaliation for WPATH's constitutionally protected speech and that Plaintiff faced irreparable harm without preliminary relief. The Court declined, however, to grant WPATH broader relief, confining its holding to the facts that gave rise to the specific CID before it.

The FTC and several states then brought a separate enforcement action against WPATH in the Northern District of Texas. They allege that WPATH violated the FTC Act's prohibitions on unfair or deceptive trade practices and false advertising. WPATH returned to this Court and sought a TRO enjoining the FTC from pursuing its Texas litigation. Plaintiff contends that the FTC's suit concerns the exact same subject matter as its previously filed pre-enforcement challenge in this Court and would frustrate the pre-existing preliminary injunction. It asserts that the FTC should proceed in this Court, not the Northern District of Texas, and it seeks an anti-suit injunction to that effect.

One of WPATH's grounds for emergency relief is its contention that halting the FTC's progress in the Northern District of Texas is necessary to protect this Court's jurisdiction over its prior injunction. The D.C. Circuit has held that anti-suit injunctions that "protect the ordering court's own jurisdiction" are more readily justified than ones whose "only purpose is to destroy" another court's jurisdiction. Where a movant can demonstrate that an anti-suit injunction is required to preserve the court's jurisdiction, then, emergency relief might well be warranted.

WPATH has not demonstrated such a threat. The Court sees no potential for "frustration of the preliminary injunction [it] has already issued" from the separate Texas litigation. However the parties characterize the ongoing action in the Northern District of Texas, it is not an attempt to enforce the CID, which the FTC has withdrawn. And, as discussed above, this Court's only decision was confined to the CID.

WPATH points out that the FTC's Texas suit attacks certain of its statements that bear a striking resemblance to the "Covered Statements" that the CID targeted, which could open it up to discovery in Texas of the very same information. But the Court's injunction did not protect WPATH from complying with all information-seeking processes—only the CID that the Court held was likely retaliatory. This Court retains its ability to consider future matters raised by any party. There is thus no threat to this Court's jurisdiction from different proceedings in a different court.

WPATH's remaining justification for emergency relief halting the Texas proceedings—that the FTC's suit is duplicative of litigation in this Court—seeks an anti-suit injunction where no jurisdictional issues are implicated. A truly compelling showing would thus be needed to warrant this Court's disrupting proceedings in a coordinate district and doing so on an emergency basis with truncated briefing and consideration.

WPATH has not made such a showing. The Court is skeptical that either Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 13(a)—which requires that parties bring related claims as compulsory counterclaims in an initial suit—or the rationale for avoiding duplicative litigation applies to actions by the Government to enforce the law. Several courts have reasoned that a plaintiff who files a pre-enforcement challenge to stop the FTC from obtaining information cannot then force the Commission to bring later enforcement actions as compulsory counterclaims in plaintiff's chosen forum.

As the Court mentioned during yesterday's hearing, linking a suit by the FTC to pre-enforcement-investigation challenges risks allowing plaintiffs "to choose the forum and pace of the litigation simply by bringing pre[-]enforcement actions." Courts have been hesitant to force government agencies to comply with a counterclaim requirement that would compress their investigative timelines and force hasty litigation decisions. This Court is thus not prepared to conclude that an action that "involve[s] assertion of rights under a Congressionally mandated enforcement scheme" is akin to a private dispute between parties that should reasonably be consolidated in one forum and time. And before one court acts to terminate another's jurisdiction, it must be on sure footing indeed.

The Court's conclusion that immediate action is unwarranted is bolstered by WPATH's inability to show that it currently faces irreparable harm. A temporary restraining order "is an emergency procedure that is appropriate only when an applicant is in need [of] immediate relief." The harm must be "great" in its impact and "certain" in its arrival to warrant preemptive action before a court can fully ascertain the merits of the parties' positions.

WPATH cites two sources of harm: violation of its First Amendment rights by the Texas litigation and monetary harm incurred by "litigating two separate cases." To find the existence of the first harm, however, this Court must hold that being subject to a suit in the Northern District of Texas is itself a violation of WPATH's First Amendment rights—else no harm would flow.

While WPATH hinges its argument on the Court's May decision, that holding extended only to the facts before it: the lack of justification for the sweeping CID the FTC issued to WPATH. The Court was not prepared in May to prejudge every permutation of the FTC's future actions as unconstitutionally retaliatory, and its role is not to do so now. Without such a holding, WPATH is subject only to an entity's standard obligation to litigate once a government agency brings a claim against it—that is, no harm at all.

Nor does the cost of maintaining two suits warrant emergency relief. The dual litigation is undoubtedly onerous to WPATH. Preliminary relief, however, requires significant monetary loss. "[T]he expense and disruption of defending oneself in protracted adjudicatory proceedings is not an irreparable harm." Nor has litigation in this Court progressed to the point where proceedings in Texas would constitute "a vexatious attempt to relitigate issues already decided." As the Court noted during yesterday's hearing, WPATH is free to cite this Court's prior Opinion in any attempt to stave off discovery in the Texas action….

John Bailey and Daniel F. Mummolo (DOJ-Civ) and Alex Potapov, Ethan D. Beck, Jonathan Abraham Cohen, and Ross Cooper Vaughan (FTC) represent defendants.

 

The post World Professional Association for Transgender Health (WPATH) Can't Go to D.C. Federal Court to Block FTC Enforcement in Texas Federal Court appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Lawsuit Against Media for Keeping People "Clueless" Thrown Out
articles.content:
纽约南区联邦地区法院法官肯尼斯·卡拉斯（Kenneth Karas）于周五就"司法问责中心诉立法记者协会"一案作出裁决：

2025年10月8日，原告埃莱娜·萨索尔（Elena Sassower）以无律师代理身份，与司法问责中心共同提起本案诉讼，指控34名被告媒体机构和新闻相关实体共谋实施"新闻欺诈"、"对真相的制度性轻率漠视"，并欺骗了购买者、捐赠者和纳税人，违反了《美国宪法第一修正案》。

法院驳回了这一主张。裁决要旨如下：

司法问责中心声称，被告等媒体机构"违反第一修正案的责任和新闻行为准则，导致司法问责中心三十余年的辛勤工作未能带来任何消除腐败的成效——而只要媒体稍稍遵守这些责任和准则，本可带来全面的消除腐败成效"。但法院认为，这种所谓的伤害过于遥远和不确定，不构成事实伤害。

司法问责中心还声称，"媒体几乎完全压制了对其工作的任何报道，只以贬低、贬损的方式提及，使其失去公众形象和认可及由此带来的一切好处"。但法院援引判例指出，"宪法上的言论自由权从未被认为包括要求记者或任何其他公民听取某人观点的权利，更不用说要求出版商在其报纸上发表这些观点的权利。"因此，由于司法问责中心对其工作获得有利报道没有法律上可主张的权利，不能以此为基础主张事实伤害。

原告还声称代表"纽约州人民"提起本案——这些人民因"新闻自由"而被蒙在鼓里，不知道选举和任命的官员如何背叛他们，三权分支公然违反就职誓言、州宪法、法律和规则，窃取金钱，制定政策却未经过立法正当程序，且存在欺诈，包括涉及选举的欺诈。

然而，法院指出，萨索尔的无律师代理身份不允许她代表除自己以外的任何人进行诉讼。此外，原告不能依靠公众受到的普遍伤害来确立诉讼资格，因为事实伤害必须是提起诉讼的个人特有的。如上所述，原告没有法律上可强制执行的权利——无论是基于第一修正案还是其他任何权威——来迫使被告报道他们选择的主题，因此不能以此为由主张自己或公众受到伤害。

关于萨索尔更具个人化的主张，法院指出：首先，原告缺乏确立诉讼资格所必需的"事实伤害"这一法律认可要素。在详细说明其所谓的伤害时，萨索尔声称"纽约司法系统的腐败以及包括许多被告在内的媒体的共谋，摧毁了她父母的生活和职业生涯"，这反过来"从她还是个孩子的时候就摧毁和毁掉了她自己的生活和职业生涯——她现在69岁了"。

然而，萨索尔父母遭受的任何伤害不能赋予她提起本诉讼的诉讼资格。法院援引判例指出，所谓的宪法侵权"完全是个人的，直接针对宪法侵权行为的直接受害者，因此基于此的诉讼理由不能为据称亲自遭受后续附带伤害的受害者家庭成员所主张。"萨索尔没有提供关于被告行为如何"摧毁和毁掉"她"的生活和职业生涯"的细节，因此没有提出足够的事实来合理地指控她遭受了具体的和特定的伤害。

"起诉媒体让人们'蒙在鼓里'的诉讼被驳回"一文首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
From Judge Kenneth Karas (S.D.N.Y.) Friday in Center for Judicial Accountability, Inc. v. Legislative Correspondents' Ass'n:

On October 8, 2025, Plaintiff Elena Sassower …, proceeding pro se, along with the Center for Judicial Accountability, Inc. … brought this Action, alleging the 34 Defendant media outlets and journalism-related entities … conspired to commit "journalistic fraud," "institutional reckless disregard for truth," and "defraud[ed] purchasers, contributors, [and] taxpayers" in violation of the First Amendment to the United States Constitution.

No, said the court; an excerpt:

[CJA] claims Defendants' "violation of First Amendment responsibilities and journalistic codes by the press, including most of the [D]efendants herein, has meant that all of [CJA]'s hard, painstaking work, spanning more than three decades, has brought no corruption-eradicating changes[ ] when even a modicum of press adherence to such responsibilities and codes … could have brought sweeping[,] corruption-eradicating changes," but this alleged injury is too remote and speculative to constitute an injury in fact….

CJA also claims that "[t]he near total press suppression of any report of [its] work, other than in minimizing, deprecating terms, deprived it of any public profile and appreciation [ ] and all the benefits flowing therefrom[,]" but "the constitutional right of free speech has never been thought to embrace a right to require a journalist or any other citizen to listen to a person's views, let alone a right to require a publisher to publish those views in his newspaper." Accordingly, because CJA has no legally cognizable right to favorable press coverage of its work, it cannot assert an injury-in-fact on this basis….

Plaintiffs [also] claim to bring this Action on behalf of the People of the State of New York who

have been kept 'clueless' by their 'free press' as to how they have been betrayed by their elected and appointed constitutional officers in their three government branches whose flagrant violations of their oaths of office, the state Constitution, laws, and rules, steal their money, … and enact and enable the enactment of policies, without legislative due process and by fraud, including as relates to elections ….

However, … Sassower's pro se status precludes her from litigating on behalf of anyone except herself. Additionally, … Plaintiffs cannot rely on harm suffered by the general public to establish standing because the injury in fact must be particularized to the individuals bringing a case.

Further, as explained above, Plaintiffs do not have a legally enforceable right—under the First Amendment or any other authority—to force Defendants to report on the topics of their choice, so they cannot claim injury to themselves or the general public on that basis.

And an excerpt as to Sassower's more personalized claim:

First, Plaintiffs lack a legally cognizable "injury in fact" as required to establish standing. In detailing her alleged injury, Sassower claims that "the corruption of New York's judiciary … and the complicity of the press, including many [of the Defendants], destroyed the lives and professional careers of her … parents," which, in turn, "destroy[ed] and derail[ed] [Sassower's] own life and career[ ] beginning when she was a child[,] and she is now 69."

However, any injury suffered by Sassower's parents does not give her standing to bring this Action because an alleged constitutional violation "is entirely personal to the direct victim of the alleged constitutional tort, and therefore 'no cause of action [based on it] may lie … for … consequent collateral injuries allegedly suffered personally by the victim's family members.'" Without providing details as to how Defendants' conduct "destroy[ed] and derail[ed]" her "life and career[,]" Sassower has not pled sufficient facts to plausibly allege she suffered a concrete and particularized injury-in-fact.

The post Lawsuit Against Media for Keeping People "Clueless" Thrown Out appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Emergency! and the Legalization of Paramedic Services
articles.content:
伦道夫·曼图斯的死讯传来时，他曾在电视剧《紧急救援！》中饰演急救员约翰尼·盖奇，这让我想起了我在加州大学洛杉矶分校（UCLA）的同事保罗·伯格曼于2007年发表的文章《紧急救援！：派一部电视剧来拯救急救服务！》。以下是文章摘录：

本文……旨在记录《紧急救援！》在推动法律变革方面的关键作用，正是这些法律变革使得在该剧播出新剧集期间，急救服务得以大规模普及……

急救服务的合法化需要在刑法和民法责任相关的法律原则方面进行重大变革。例如，各州法律均规定，非医师行医属于无证执业罪。这些法律使急救服务变得不可行，因为急救员可能执行的许多医疗功能都属于行医行为。即使急救员接受过培训并能证明自己具备执行急救任务的专业能力，仍可能根据这些法律受到刑事起诉。

民事责任规则也对急救服务的发展构成了重大障碍。急救员可能因患者因急救员疏忽而遭受的任何损害而被判令支付赔偿。由于在大多数司法管辖区，执行非法医疗程序本身即构成过失，因此原告要证明急救员存在疏忽并非难事。除了上述“本身即构成过失”的推理外，急救员还可能被要求遵守与医生相同的诊疗标准。

1973年《紧急医疗服务系统法》的参议院报告总结了急救服务发展所面临的法律挑战。报告指出：“该法案要求国务卿对紧急情况下有效提供医疗服务的法律障碍进行研究……在某些州，紧急医疗服务的提供受到执照、医疗事故和责任方面僵硬法律的制约。”

除了实现急救服务发展和扩张所需的法律变革外，许多医师和护士团体也对急救员概念表示反对。例如，1969年，两名研究人员在威斯康星州调查了1300多名医生。研究人员询问这些医生是否愿意允许急救员执行与其专业领域密切相关的医疗任务。大多数医生给出了否定回答。

医生们的专业协会——美国医学会（AMA）建议推迟立法，这是反对者惯用的策略，他们可能不想直接挑战某项改革。美国医学会的立场是，在授权急救服务的立法通过之前，需要进行更多试验。然而，个别医生则没有那么克制。例如，伊利诺伊州的一位医生认为：“这套移动医疗的东西充满了危险……如果有人只经过几周培训就接手你丈夫的工作，你会作何感想？”……

护士们往往比医生更强烈地反对急救服务的合法化，因为急救员对许多护士和护理团体构成了潜在的“地盘”问题。急救员的合法化带来了风险，即先前被视为护理职业一部分的职责可能会转移到急救员身上，尤其是在新的医疗技术方面。因此，若干护理协会最初公开反对急救服务的合法化……

由于这些挑战，尽管将急救员纳入改进后的紧急服务的希望初现端倪，但现实是，到1960年代末，实际的急救服务几乎不存在。截至1971年，整个美国仅有12个急救小组在运作。然而，由于缺乏立法授权，其中一些小组的运作在一定程度上处于半地下状态。

幸运的是，这12个急救小组中有两个设在洛杉矶——世界娱乐之都。加利福尼亚州是第一个颁布全面急救服务授权法的州，这两个洛杉矶急救小组是根据该法律运作的，即1970年的《威德斯沃斯-汤森急救法》。

鉴于急救服务的不稳定地位，《威德斯沃斯-汤森急救法》只是一个试验性试点项目。该法在颁布两年后自动失效，且仅授权在“人口超过600万”的县提供急救服务，这意味着《威德斯沃斯-汤森急救法》授权的急救服务仅限于洛杉矶县。尽管该法在态度上谨慎而踌躇，但它为《紧急救援！》的诞生提供了推动力……

在加利福尼亚州颁布《威德斯沃斯-汤森急救法》后不久，大众文化就开始为急救服务的发展做出贡献。用洛杉矶县消防队长吉姆·佩奇的话说，他是洛杉矶早期急救员培训项目的参与者，“1971年5月11日对急救员理念而言意义重大。”就在那天，曾参与《法网恢恢》制作的老牌电视制片人罗伯特·锡纳德，与杰克·韦布（同样因《法网恢恢》而闻名）会见了佩奇队长以及其他洛杉矶县消防部门官员，讨论开发一部以消防部门救援人员事迹为原型的新电视剧。

锡纳德的最初概念侧重于物理救援场景。他请佩奇队长帮助开发可以在每周节目中呈现的救援场景。佩奇队长开始收集故事创意，但很快认为仅关注物理救援场景太过局限，并指出：“能够呈现的塌方、建筑坍塌和类似灾难的种类毕竟有限，否则可能会出现令人乏味的重复。”正如命运安排的那样，佩奇队长熟悉洛杉矶县的试验性急救运作。此外，在与锡纳德初次会面后不久，佩奇队长被晋升为消防大队长，从而将县里的两个急救小组置于其指挥之下。大队长佩奇随后向锡纳德建议，将节目焦点从物理救援改为展示急救员的工作。

锡纳德对佩奇关于急救员的提议最初反应冷淡，但很快便成为支持者。锡纳德成为急救小组所在消防站的常客，他多次陪同急救员出诊。1971年9月，韦布和锡纳德与全国广播公司（NBC）电视网签约，制作一部以急救员工作为主题的两小时首映电影。该电影名为《紧急救援！》，于1971年12月首次在洛杉矶放映，1972年1月在全国播出。

在那个没有有线电视、没有卫星转播的时代，大多数美国观众只能收看到三个全国电视网和或许一两个地方台的节目，《紧急救援！》成为一部非常受欢迎的剧集，在NBC每周六晚间播出。《紧急救援！》的新一小时剧集持续播出至1977年，总共制作了129集一小时剧集和6部两小时电视电影。该剧经常位列全美收视前十名，每集平均吸引约3000万全国观众。

《紧急救援！》的流行恰好与急救服务的迅猛扩张同步。如上所述，1971年仅有12个急救小组（其中一些合法性存疑）在少数几个州运作。1974年，福特总统签署了《紧急医疗服务系统法》。该法设立了资金，各地方社区可以申请用于建立或改善其紧急医疗服务系统。该法案的参议院报告确定了社区获得资助所需满足的要求。在《紧急救援！》播出不到两年后，急救服务已从稀缺且有时非法的资源转变为获得联邦资金的必要条件：

对于急救辅助人员的充分培训的重要性，无论怎样强调都不为过。在大多数情况下，他们是第一个到达紧急现场的人……这些人在紧急现场……能够提供挽救生命的护理和使用对挽救患者生命、防止其严重残疾至关重要的复杂设备。

在联邦资金的帮助下，到1975年底（《紧急救援！》播出的最初三年），美国50个州中有46个州已颁布法律授权急救服务。到本年代末，约一半的美国人居住在距离急救小组十分钟路程的范围内。

分析《紧急救援！》对急救服务快速扩张的影响，必须首先承认一个众所周知的观点：“相关性不等于因果关系。”也就是说，《紧急救援！》可能并未在急救服务发展中发挥独立作用，但其流行可能只是反映了推动急救服务在全国推广的同一股兴趣。然而，大量证据支持这样的结论：这部电视剧是推动法律变革的主要因素，正是这些法律变革使急救服务得以发展和扩张……

随着时间推移，《紧急救援！》继续被公认为急救项目发展的主要影响因素。例如，2000年，美国急诊医师学会主席承认：“电视剧《紧急救援！》在提高公众对紧急医疗的认识以及促进现代紧急医疗服务早期历史和发展方面发挥了重要作用。”……从2000年的角度回顾，长滩市消防局副局长斯科特·卡明斯讲述了同样的故事：

我记得自己10岁时看《紧急救援！》，它绝对是我进入消防服务行业的推动力……在1970年代初，当地消防部门几乎没有任何紧急救援小组，正是这部电视剧让人们想要在社区中建立这样的团队，同时也使其成为一条有吸引力的职业道路……

文章《紧急救援！》与急救服务的合法化率先发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
News of the death of Randolph Mantooth, who played paramedic Johnny Gage on Emergency! reminded me of my UCLA colleague Paul Bergman's 2007 article, Emergency!: Send a TV Show to Rescue Parademic Services!; an excerpt:

This essay … seeks to document the pivotal role of Emergency! in producing an array of legal changes that resulted in an explosion in the availability of paramedic services during the time that the TV show aired new episodes….

The legalization of paramedic services required major changes in legal principles relating to both criminal and civil liability. For example, laws in all states made it a crime for non-physicians to practice medicine without a license. These laws rendered paramedic services unfeasible due to the fact that many of the medical functions that paramedics could have performed constituted the practice of medicine. Paramedics could be criminally prosecuted under these laws even if they had undergone training and could demonstrate expertise in carrying out their paramedic tasks.

Civil liability rules also posed significant challenges for the development of paramedic services. Paramedics could be ordered to pay damages for any harms that patients incurred due to paramedic negligence. A showing of negligence was not a significant hurdle for plaintiffs to overcome since, in most jurisdictions, performing illegal medical procedures constituted negligence per se. Even apart from the negligence per se reasoning, paramedics were likely to be held to the same standard of care as physicians.

The Senate Report on the Emergency Medical Services Systems Act of 1973 summarized the legal challenges facing the development of paramedic services. The report stated that "[t]he reported bill directs the Secretary to conduct a study of the legal barriers to the effective delivery of medical care under emergency conditions …. The provision of emergency medical services is affected in some states by inflexible laws on licensure, malpractice and liability."

Adding to the need for legal changes that would have to occur if paramedic services were to develop and expand was the opposition demonstrated by many physician and nurse groups to the paramedic concept. For example, two researchers writing in 1969 surveyed over 1,300 Wisconsin physicians. The researchers asked the physicians whether they would be willing to permit paramedics to perform duties closely related to their medical specialties. The majority of physicians responded in the negative.

The physicians' professional association, the American Medical Association (AMA), recommended delay, the favorite tactic of opponents who might not want to directly challenge a reform. The AMA's position was that more experimentation was needed before legislation authorizing paramedic services was enacted. Individual doctors, however, were not so restrained. For example, one Illinois doctor thought that "[t]his whole mobile medical thing is loaded with danger …. How would you like it if someone, after only a few weeks' training, took over your husband's job?" …

Nurses often opposed the legalization of paramedic services more stridently than doctors because paramedics presented a potential "turf" issue for many nurses and nursing groups. Legalization of paramedics created a risk that duties previously regarded as part of the nursing profession would be shifted to paramedics, especially with respect to new medical technology. Thus, a number of nursing associations initially went on record as opposing the legalization of paramedic services….

As a result of these challenges and despite the glimmerings of hope for including paramedics in improved emergency services, the reality was that actual paramedic services were virtually non-existent at the end of the 1960s. As of 1971, only twelve paramedic units were in existence in the entire United States. However, in the absence of legislative authority, several of these units operated somewhat clandestinely.

As luck would have it, however, two of these twelve paramedic units were based in Los Angeles, the show-biz capital of the world. California was the first state to enact a comprehensive law authorizing paramedic services, and the two Los Angeles paramedic units operated under the authority of that law, the Wedworth-Townsend Paramedic Act of 1970.

Reflecting the uncertain toehold of paramedic services, the Wedworth-Townsend Paramedic Act was merely an experimental pilot program. The law expired automatically two years after its enactment, and it authorized paramedic services only in counties "with a population of over 6,000,000" people, meaning that the paramedic services that Wedworth-Townsend authorized were limited to Los Angeles County. Despite its rudimentary and hesitant approach, however, the Wedworth-Townsend Paramedic Act provided the impetus for Emergency! …

Popular culture's contribution to the development of paramedic services began shortly after the enactment of California's Wedworth-Townsend Paramedic Act. In the words of Los Angeles County Fire Captain Jim Page, who was involved in the early paramedic training programs in Los Angeles, "May 11, 1971 was a day of great significance to the paramedic concept." On that date veteran television producer Robert Cinader, working with Jack Webb (of Dragnet fame) met with Captain Page and other Los Angeles County Fire Department officials to discuss the development of a new television series based on the exploits of fire department rescue personnel.

Cinader's initial concept focused on physical rescue situations. Cinader asked Captain Page for help in developing rescue scenarios that could be depicted in a weekly series. Captain Page began collecting story ideas, but soon came to believe that the focus on physical rescue situations was too limiting and stated, "[t]here [are] only so many kinds of cave-ins, building collapses, and similar calamities that could be depicted without encountering potentially boring similarities." As fate would have it, Captain Page was familiar with Los Angeles County's experimental paramedic operations. Moreover, shortly after his initial meeting with Cinader, Captain Page was promoted to the office of Battalion Chief, and as a result had the County's two paramedic units under his command. Battalion Chief Page then suggested to Cinader that the focus of the show be changed from physical rescues to depicting paramedics in action.

Cinader's reaction to Page's paramedic proposal was initially cool, but he quickly became a believer. Cinader became a fixture in the fire stations that housed the paramedic units, and he accompanied the paramedics on numerous emergency calls. In September 1971, Webb and Cinader signed a contract with the National Broadcasting Company (NBC) television network to produce a two-hour world premiere movie based on the work of the paramedics. The movie, titled Emergency!, was first shown in Los Angeles in December 1971 and aired nationally in January 1972.

In the pre-cable, pre-satellite era when most American television viewers had access to programming only on three national networks and perhaps one or two local stations, Emergency! became a very popular series that ran on Saturday nights on NBC. New one-hour episodes of Emergency! continued to air through 1977, totaling 129 one-hour episodes and 6 two-hour Movies of the Week. Emergency! was often ranked among the ten most-watched shows in the country, and its national audience averaged about 30 million viewers per episode.

The popularity of Emergency! coincided with the explosive expansion of paramedic services. As mentioned above, twelve paramedic units (some of dubious legality) were operating in a few states in 1971. In 1974, President Ford signed the Emergency Medical Services Systems Act into law. That law established funds for which local communities could apply for the purposes of establishing or improving their emergency medical services systems. The Senate Report on the Act identified the requirements that communities were required to meet in order to receive funding. Barely two years after Emergency! went on the air, paramedic services had moved from a scarce and sometimes illegal resource to a requirement for receiving federal funds:

The importance of adequate training of the paraprofessional, who, in most instances, is the first person at the scene of the emergency, cannot be overemphasized …. These individuals on the emergency scene … are capable of providing lifesaving care and utilizing complex equipment essential to save the patient from death and protect him from serious disability.

With the aid of federal funding, by the end of 1975 (during the first 3 years that Emergency! was on the air), forty-six of the fifty American states had enacted laws authorizing paramedic services. By the end of the decade, about one-half of all Americans lived within ten minutes of a paramedic unit.

An analysis of Emergency!'s influence on the rapid expansion of paramedic services must begin with the acknowledgement of the familiar bromide that "correlation does not equal causation." That is, Emergency! may not have played an independent role in the development of paramedic services, but rather its popularity may have reflected the same interest in paramedic services that produced their spread throughout the country. However, ample evidence supports a conclusion that the TV show was a primary factor that fueled the legal changes that allowed paramedic services to develop and expand….

As time has passed, Emergency! continues to be recognized as a primary influence on the development of paramedic programs. For example, in the year 2000, the president of the American College of Emergency Physicians recognized "the significant role the TV series Emergency! played in raising public awareness of emergency care and promoting the early history and development of modern EMS." … Looking backwards from the year 2000, Long Beach Deputy Fire Commissioner Scott Kamins tells much the same story:

I remember watching that show [Emergency!] when I was 10 years old, and it is definitely what pushed me into fire services …. There were hardly any emergency service units in local fire departments back then in the early 1970's, and it was this show that made people want to have such teams in their community while at the same time making it an attractive career path….

The post <i>Emergency!</i> and the Legalization of Paramedic Services appeared first on Reason.com.
